claim.hypotheses_location: Abstract and Introduction: p. 725–726 (e.g., 'In four experiments, we hypothesize and demonstrate that the more a prospect’s frame is associated with risk ... the lower people’s WTP. We hypothesize that WTA, in contrast, will not be affected by how the prospect is framed ...').
claim.statement_location: Title and Abstract, p. 725 (e.g., title: 'Framing Influences Willingness to Pay but Not Willingness to Accept'; abstract first paragraph).

data.source: Online experiments with human participants recruited via Amazon Mechanical Turk (MTurk); incentive-compatible procedures used in some studies with real payoffs.
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: n=119; Experiment 2: n=418; Experiment 3: n=533; Experiment 4: n=605 (pretests also conducted).
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant responses (WTP/WTA amounts; ratings for pretests).
data.notes: Participants completed instructional manipulation checks and comprehension questions; exclusion based on failed comprehension/attention checks; random assignment to conditions; both real and hypothetical payoffs were used; outcomes typically two-outcome 50/50 prospects ($50/$100 or variants); analyses sometimes restricted to participants correctly identifying the lowest possible outcome.

method.description: Across four MTurk experiments, participants stated willingness to pay (WTP) or willingness to accept (WTA) for risky or riskless prospects labeled with different frames (e.g., lottery, raffle, coin flip, gamble, gift certificate, voucher). The studies examined how framing and outcome levels (low/high) affect WTP vs. WTA. Experiment 1 tested lottery vs. gift card frames with real payoffs. Experiment 2 orthogonally manipulated frame (gift certificate vs. lottery), outcome levels (low/high values), and endowment status (buyer vs. seller). Experiment 3 generalized frames to six labels and compared effects on WTP vs. WTA. Experiment 4 orthogonally manipulated objective uncertainty (risky prospect vs. certain low outcome) and the six frames to test framing effects independent of objective risk and explore conditions producing the uncertainty effect.
steps: Recruit participants on MTurk; obtain informed consent and demographics.
Administer instructional manipulation check and comprehension question regarding lowest possible outcome; pre-register exclusion rules (applied consistently across experiments).

Randomly assign participants to framing conditions (e.g., gift certificate, voucher, lottery, raffle, coin flip, gamble) and, where applicable, to outcome structures (e.g., $25/$100, $50/$100, $50/$200) and endowment status (buyer/WTP vs. seller/WTA).

Present incentive-compatible or hypothetical valuation tasks: elicit WTP for buying or WTA for selling the described prospect (two-outcome 50/50 risky prospects; certain low-outcome prospects in Exp. 4).
Record WTP/WTA amounts; in pretests, collect risk/uncertainty association ratings for frames; in Exp. 4, collect processing disfluency ratings.
Apply exclusion criteria (e.g., failed comprehension); analyze only participants who correctly identified lowest possible outcome (primary analyses also reported including all).

Conduct statistical analyses (t-tests, ANOVA/ANCOVA, nonparametric tests such as Mann–Whitney and Kruskal–Wallis, chi-square for comprehension rates); report effect sizes (e.g., partial eta-squared) and p-values.

Interpret results regarding differential sensitivity of WTP (to frame risk association and low outcomes) and WTA (to low and high outcomes, but not to frame), and assess conditions for the uncertainty effect.
method.models: Between-subjects ANOVA (main effects and interactions), independent-samples t-tests, nonparametric tests (Mann–Whitney, Kruskal–Wallis H), chi-square tests for comprehension checks; effect sizes reported as partial eta-squared (ηp²).
method.outcome_variable: Primary: WTP (willingness to pay) and WTA (willingness to accept) amounts in USD. Secondary: risk/uncertainty association ratings of frames (pretests), processing disfluency ratings (Exp. 4).
method.independent_variables: Frame label (gift certificate, voucher, lottery, raffle, coin flip, gamble); endowment status (buyer vs. seller); outcome structure of risky prospect (e.g., low=$25 or $50; high=$100 or $200); objective uncertainty (risky 50/50 vs. certain low outcome).
method.tools_software: MTurk for participant recruitment and survey delivery; specific statistical software not stated.

results.summary: Across four experiments, framing a prospect with labels associated with risk (lottery, raffle, coin flip, gamble) substantially reduced WTP relative to frames weakly associated with risk (gift certificate, voucher), while framing had little to no effect on WTA. Buyers (WTP) were sensitive to the risky prospect’s lowest possible outcome and to the frame’s risk association, but not to the highest outcome; sellers (WTA) were sensitive to both low and high outcomes but insensitive to frame. These differential effects were observed with real and hypothetical payoffs and persisted even when objective uncertainty was absent (Exp. 4), indicating that framing can independently depress WTP. Frame mismatches (risky prospect labeled with high-risk frame vs. certain low outcome labeled with low-risk frame) produced the uncertainty effect, whereby the risky prospect was valued less than its worst certain outcome; when frames matched, the uncertainty effect largely disappeared.

metadata.original_paper_title: Framing Influences Willingness to Pay but Not Willingness to Accept
